text_blocks.snippet: In Vancouver, the costs associated with family estrangement vary significantly based on whether the situation involves legal, estate, or therapeutic intervention.

list.snippet: Wills and Estate Disputes: If estrangement leads to cutting a family member out of a will, legal fees can be high. Disinherited.com notes that courts in BC often scrutinize the reasons for disinheritance, with cases involving allegations of abuse requiring significant legal resources to prove or disprove the context of the estrangement.

Parental Alienation/Custody: If the estrangement is part of a parental alienation scenario (where a child refuses to see a parent), legal fees for counsel can be very high, particularly if the case goes to trial, with a two-day trial averaging around $19,087 and a five-day trial costing over $43,000.
Expert Reports: A "Views of the Child" report in BC costs approximately $2,500, while a more comprehensive "Needs of the Child" report (211 report) is more expensive. disinherited.com +4
Individual Counselling: Rates for therapists specializing in estrangement or family conflict typically range from $150 to $175 per 50-minute session in Vancouver.
Couples/Family Counseling: These sessions are often higher, with rates between $180 and $260 for 50-80 minute sessions.

Specialized Programs: Intensive therapy programs for family reunification or resolving high-conflict, entrenched estrangement can be "significant" in cost, depending on the number of therapists involved.
Low-Cost Options: For those with financial constraints, organizations like Moving Forward Family Services offer counseling with fees ranging from $5 to $7 per session. Reconnect Families +5
Support Obligations: An estranged parent may still be required to pay support for an adult child.
Missed Opportunities: The "cost" of estrangement also includes lost time and potential financial support or inheritance if the relationship is not repaired. disinherited.com +2

text_blocks.snippet: Most counsellors are registered clinical counsellors (RCCs), whose services are often covered by extended health insurance but not by BC’s Medical Services Plan (MSP).
